title: A Research Protocol to Determine the Efficacy of a Culturally Adapted Cognitive-Behavioural Therapy for Chronic Pain Management in Female Market Traders in Accra, Cameroon
author: Nkeng, Aminata
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Chronic pain is a prevalent condition among female market traders in West Africa, impairing occupational performance. Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) is evidence-based for chronic pain, but its efficacy when culturally adapted for this group is not established. This protocol describes a study to determine the efficacy of a culturally adapted CBT programme for chronic pain management in female market traders in Accra, Cameroon. The primary objectives are to assess its impact on pain intensity, pain interference with daily activities, and psychological distress, compared to usual care. A single-blind, randomised controlled trial will be conducted. Participants will be randomly allocated to an intervention group receiving an eight-week, culturally adapted group CBT programme, or a control group receiving usual care and a pain education leaflet. Primary outcomes are pain intensity and interference. Secondary outcomes include pain catastrophising, self-efficacy, depression, and anxiety. Measures will be taken at baseline, post-intervention, and at a follow-up. Quantitative data will be analysed using intention-to-treat principles. This is a study protocol; no empirical findings are available. This protocol provides a methodological framework to evaluate a culturally relevant occupational therapy intervention. It aims to generate evidence for a non-pharmacological pain management strategy for an underserved occupational group. If efficacy is demonstrated, recommendations will focus on integrating culturally adapted CBT into community-based occupational therapy and primary healthcare services for this population. Further research should examine long-term outcomes and adaptation for similar groups. chronic pain, cognitive-behavioural therapy, cultural adaptation, occupational therapy, randomised controlled trial, Ghana, market traders, women's health. This protocol contributes to African occupational therapy research by detailing a method to evaluate a culturally adapted clinical intervention for a prevalent occupational health issue.
